items.rationale: Seen as the breakout consumer use case that bootstraps communities and gives creators ownership and far better economics than web2; maturing toward a Pokemon-cards-style collectibles model rather than pure scarcity.
items.age_note: Core thesis since mid-2021; continuously restated through late 2022, so current not stale.

citations.quote: NFTs add a new layer of value — digital ownership — that didn't exist in a credible way before NFTs.
the long run digital collectibles business model will look more like @Pokemon cards than the ultra-scarcity pervasive in NFTs today.

items.rationale: Positioned as the structural answer to centralized-finance failures: transparent, composable, no intermediary trust. Conviction reinforced rather than shaken by 2022 collapses.
items.age_note: Bullish since 'DeFi summer' framing 2021; reaffirmed strongest post-FTX in Dec 2022.

citations.quote: Do the collapses of FTX, Celsius, and Terra signal the end of crypto? No, but they do show why we need #DeFi.
DeFi shows it's possible to build financial services that are inclusive, fair, transparent, and composable.

items.rationale: Viewed as a foundational breakthrough — as transformative as smart contracts — unlocking scaling (rollups) plus privacy without sacrificing verifiability; backing Aztec, Aleo, zkSync.
items.age_note: Growing conviction across 2022; latest restatement Dec 2022 (Aztec).

citations.quote: i still think people underestimate how transformational zero knowledge proofs are
Encryption & privacy will be essential for blockchains to broaden their utility and gain widespread adoption.

items.rationale: Optimistic and ZK rollups seen as the path to scale Ethereum and offer a trustless alternative to custodial exchanges; diversity of rollup approaches valued.

citations.quote: @optimismPBC is a huge unlock for #L222. Bringing back the fun of DeFi summer.
rollups like are actually live today with production code, which is kind of amazing when you think about it

items.rationale: The gap between web2's ~100% take rates and web3's ~2.5% is framed as the central disruption opportunity for creators and startups.

citations.quote: OpenSea keeps 2.5%. On Instagram, creators make nothing (unless they make deals outside Instagram).
web3 takes us back to the internet's original vision... power and money flow to the edges instead of centralized intermediaries.

items.rationale: Belief that open, user-owned, portable-data social protocols (Farcaster) will displace captive web2 platforms; 'regulate apps not protocols' thesis underpins it.

citations.quote: The decentralization of social media is going to be very obvious to the masses in retrospect.
Farcaster is the neutral platform we wanted all along.

items.rationale: Crypto incentives can bootstrap real-world networks bottom-up against telco incumbents — held up as proof crypto goes beyond currency.
items.age_note: Strong 2021 thesis (Helium 100k hotspots); last restated Mar 2022 (Nova Labs), not heavily revisited since.

citations.quote: Helium's use of crypto incentives spurred the world's fastest-growing wireless network – now 500k+ Hotspots
The Web 3 playbook: using token incentives to bootstrap new networks.

items.rationale: User-owned game assets (NFTs as building blocks) and on-chain game logic seen as next breakout; backing Halliday, expecting many launches.

citations.quote: Crypto-enabled games will unlock new opportunities for players to create & own the future of those worlds with NFTs
if you want truly decentralized crypto games you have to put logic on-chain.

items.rationale: Still considered foundational coordination primitives for web3, but expectations were explicitly lowered in 2022 and legal/governance work is seen as unfinished.
items.age_note: Very bullish 2021 ('10,000x faster than corporations'); cooled mid-2022 to 'city governments, not dynamic startups' (2022-06-23).

citations.quote: DAOs are a groundbreaking way for people with shared interests to collaborate towards common goals
Most DAOs should be viewed as city governments, not dynamic startups.

items.rationale: Emerging frontier — machines that can own (crypto) plus think (AI); early but flagged as a new design space.
items.age_note: Idea surfaced 2021 (Kosen, Runloop); crispest statement Dec 2022 — still nascent/extrapolative.

citations.quote: AI = how machines can think / crypto = how machines can own / AI + crypto = how machines can think on their own

items.rationale: The single largest, most-repeated concern: lack of SEC guidance since 2019, risk of regulating protocols (not apps), and enforcement driving builders offshore. Existential if protocols get regulated.
items.age_note: Persistent since 2021; intensified and most-cited concern through Dec 2022.

citations.quote: We can't regulate protocols and keep them decentralized and autonomous... That would end web3.
the SEC doesn't care where you are and will go after you if you offer products to Americans
The SEC's regulatory authority isn't the problem, it's the SEC's unwillingness to use its existing authority

items.rationale: FTX, Celsius, Voyager, BlockFi collapses framed as failures of trust-based centralization, not the technology; custody itself seen as a liability.
items.age_note: Sharpened acutely Nov 2022 with FTX; consistent with earlier 'not your keys' framing.

citations.quote: Shoutout to the CeFi players out there demonstrating unequivocally why trust inherently needs to be regulated differently than trustless.
custody is a liability.

items.rationale: Terra/UST collapse attributed to bad (endogenous) collateral; uncollateralized designs dismissed outright, though well-collateralized decentralized stablecoins are defended.
items.age_note: Crystallized at Terra collapse (May 2022); reinforced Aug & Dec 2022.

citations.quote: Uncollateralized stablecoins are basically just meme coins with no potential upside.
bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra, not 'algorithmic stablecoins.'

items.rationale: Repeatedly named the biggest direct risk to web3 participants; project hype-cycles/roadmaps likened to unregistered securities offerings.

citations.quote: Fraud continues to be the biggest risk to web3 participants

items.rationale: Selling tokens to fund development / 'utility token' framing seen as legally dead after SEC actions (LBRY, Ripple); only decentralization or airdrops are safe paths.
items.age_note: Caution since 2021; declared 'final nail in the coffin' Nov 2022 (LBRY).

citations.quote: Result of the LBRY case should be the final nail in the coffin of the 'utility token' approach for most projects.
Don't sell tokens to U.S. persons for fundraising purposes.

items.rationale: Direct/coin-voting democracy seen as unscalable and capturable by small motivated groups; missing 'free press' and delegate incentives flagged as unsolved.

citations.quote: you simply can't govern through direct democracy at any kind of meaningful scale

items.rationale: Deployed SNARKs running at ~80 bits (or lower) security; worry that prioritizing performance erodes trust and risks misappropriated funds.

citations.quote: if we prioritize performance over security, we may cause people to lose trust in the technology

items.rationale: Research-level concerns that long-range attacks lack a complete solution and that staking + client concentration centralizes validators.
items.age_note: Raised in mid/late-2022 research talks; researcher-level, not house-wide.

citations.quote: people are concerned and trying to figure out how to solve it and they just have mitigations but none of them solves the problem completely

items.rationale: Native mobile crypto UX is throttled by app-store rules and 30% take rates, crippling consumer onboarding.

citations.quote: Apple will cripple you, block you, or take your lunch if you try to offer a native experience.

items.rationale: L1 transaction throughput stayed roughly flat; the Merge explicitly does not lower fees, so high gas remains a near-term constraint until rollups scale.
items.age_note: Flagged 2021; clarified Sept 2022 that the Merge does not reduce fees.

citations.quote: the merge will reduce transaction fees on Ethereum. So at a high level, it unfortunately doesn't.
the number of transactions processed by miners has been roughly flat at ~13/sec.

items.rationale: The clear leader by developer mindshare, fees, and economic security; the Merge seen as making it materially superior (energy, finality, security budget).
items.age_note: Consistently the house's preferred L1 through 2022; bullish through the Merge (Sept 2022).

citations.quote: @ethereum is the clear leader. Its overwhelming mindshare among web3 devs
I believe Ethereum will be a far superior blockchain than it was before.

items.rationale: Valued as risk tokens that underwrite their protocols; MakerDAO cited as the example where token governance genuinely works.

citations.quote: the role of MKR and AAVE/COMP as the risk token that underwrites the protocol
MakerDAO is probably one of the examples where this actually works.

items.rationale: Flagship proof that token incentives bootstrap real-world infrastructure networks bottom-up.
items.age_note: Heavily promoted 2021; last meaningful mention Mar 2022 (Nova Labs), not restated since — persisted.

citations.quote: Helium's use of crypto incentives spurred the world's fastest-growing wireless network – now 500k+ Hotspots

items.rationale: Held up as the model for a healthy token launch (broad airdrop, DAO-led, no issuer market-making) and as web3 identity infrastructure.
items.age_note: Praised Dec 2021 as a 'case study' token launch; not restated in 2022 — persisted.

citations.quote: @ensdomains and $ENS should be a case study on how to conduct a great token launch.

items.rationale: Collapsed due to endogenous/insufficient collateral; used as the cautionary example of an extremely undercollateralized design.
items.age_note: Collapse May 2022; still cited Dec 2022 as the contrast to well-collateralized Maker.

citations.quote: Terra being extremely undercollateralized
bad collateral is the root cause of recent collapses like Terra

items.rationale: Tied to the FTX collapse; the token was viewed skeptically in legal circles well before its failure.

citations.quote: FTT was widely viewed with skepticism in the legal community, which has consistently been advising against FTX's approach in the US.

items.rationale: Subject of the SEC's effort to deem it a security; team expects an unfavorable outcome and treats it as outside the safe (decentralized) path.

citations.quote: the LBRY result doesn't portend a good result for Ripple.
By 2019, basically everyone knew not to sell tokens to US persons

items.rationale: Dismissed as lacking fundamental economic basis to thrive.
items.age_note: Stated Nov 2021, not restated since — persisted.

citations.quote: its provenance as a meme leaves it without much of a fundamental economic reason to thrive.

risk_regime.rationale: Despite the 2022 bear market and the Terra/Celsius/FTX collapses, the house maintains high structural conviction: it raised a $4.5B fund in May 2022 declaring a 'golden era of web3,' frames the downturn as a normal price-innovation-cycle trough where building continues, and reads the failures as vindicating decentralized/DeFi approaches rather than crypto itself. Posture is therefore long-term risk-on with sustained capital deployment, but tactically defensive — preoccupied with regulatory threat, CeFi contagion, fraud, and a flight to quality (decentralization, collateralization, self-custody) rather than broad speculative exposure.

notes: Recency weighting: the corpus runs Jul 2021–Dec 2022, so post-Terra (May) and post-FTX (Nov) statements were weighted most heavily. Notable reversals/cooling: DAO enthusiasm cooled from '10,000x better than corporations' (2021) to 'city governments, not dynamic startups' (Jun 2022); the 'utility token' fundraising path moved from open question to declared dead (LBRY, Nov 2022). A persistent nuance: the team sharply distinguishes 'trustless' (DeFi/protocols — defended) from 'trust-based' (CeFi/intermediaries — blamed) when assigning blame for 2022's blowups. Provenance caveats: several research-talk views (PoS long-range attacks, SNARK security, AMM loss-versus-rebalancing, consensus design) are individual a16z-crypto researchers' positions surfaced in lectures, not necessarily firm-wide house calls — labeled grounded but lower-conviction. Token-level views are sparse: the firm comments far more on sectors/protocols than on specific token prices, so token entries are inferred from protocol/launch commentary and are lower-confidence than the sector reads. Conviction scores are intensity of expressed enthusiasm/concern, not probability estimates.
